summary: Problem 4 concerns proving k-dilation inequalities for maps between rectangles in higher-dimensional Euclidean space, but the current proof attempt is only ~12% complete. While the setup and definitions are solid, the core analytic and geometric arguments establishing the dilation bounds remain unwritten, leaving the proof far from finished.
problems: The central mathematical argument bounding the k-dilation of maps between rectangles is essentially absent; only setup and definitions exist.
No control has been established over how the map distorts k-dimensional volumes, which is the crux of any k-dilation estimate.
The relationship between rectangle aspect ratios and the achievable dilation constant has not been derived or stated quantitatively.
Lack of a clear proof strategy (e.g., direct construction, calibration/cohomological obstruction, or coarea/min-max argument) for the lower/upper bound.
Verifier flags ~88% of the proof as missing, indicating no key lemma has yet been proven.
highlights: The problem restatement, setup, and definitions are correct and considered solid by the verifier, providing a reliable foundation.
The single open issue is well-scoped and actively in progress, with a verifier already engaged to give feedback.

suggested_todos.title: Choose and document a concrete proof strategy for the k-dilation bound (construction vs. obstruction argument)
Prove the core lemma controlling k-dimensional volume distortion under maps between rectangles
Derive the explicit dependence of the dilation constant on rectangle dimensions/aspect ratios
Establish matching upper and lower bounds to make the inequality sharp (or note where sharpness fails)
Re-submit the expanded draft to verifier-agent and target raising completeness above 50%
